course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Bases, Dimension, Column Space, and Null Space
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to find bases for column spaces and null spaces, use pivot columns correctly, and explain dimension using rank and nullity.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Write: basis = spanning set with no redundancy.
Write: rank = number of pivots, nullity = number of free variables.
Emphasize: for Col(A), use pivot columns from the original A, not the RREF columns.
Do Problem 1 slowly. It is the most important one.
If time is short, do Problem 1 and Problem 3.
sayThisFirst: A basis is a clean, non-redundant way to describe a space.
Dimension means how many basis vectors you need.
For a column space basis, find the pivot columns in RREF, then go back to the original matrix.
boardPlan: At the top, write: basis = span + independent.
Write the rank-nullity formula: rank(A)+nullity(A)=number of columns of A.
Explain that RREF is for finding pivots and equations, but original columns are used for Col(A).
Use Problem 1 to find Col(A), Nul(A), rank, and nullity from one matrix.
Use Problem 2 to show coordinates in a basis.
Use Problem 3 to show a subspace basis from equations.

keyFormulas.name: Basis
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: A basis describes a space with the fewest clean directions.

keyFormulas.name: Dimension
keyFormulas.latex: \dim(V)=\text{number of vectors in a basis for }V
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Dimension is the number of independent directions.

keyFormulas.name: Rank-nullity
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: For an m by n matrix, pivots plus free variables equals the number of columns.

keyFormulas.name: Column space basis
keyFormulas.latex: \text{Use pivot columns of the original matrix }A.
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Do not use the RREF columns as the final column-space basis.

commonMistakes: Using columns of RREF as a basis for Col(A). Use pivot columns from the original A.
Forgetting that null space vectors have one entry for each column of A.
Confusing rank with the number of rows.
Confusing nullity with the number of zero rows.
Calling a spanning set a basis before checking redundancy.
notationWarnings: For null space, solve Ax=0, not Ax=b.
For coordinates in a basis B, write [v]_B to show these are coordinates, not the standard vector.

problems.title: Column space and null space from RREF
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&2&-1\\2&1&5&0\\0&1&1&2\\1&-1&1&-3\end{bmatrix},\qquad \operatorname{rref}(A)=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&2&-1\\0&1&1&2\\0&0&0&0\\0&0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\]
Find a basis for \(\operatorname{Col}(A)\), a basis for \(\operatorname{Nul}(A)\), \(\operatorname{rank}(A)\), and \(\operatorname{nullity}(A)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: the RREF tells us where the pivots are. But the column-space basis comes from the original matrix.

drawInstructions: Draw the RREF matrix and circle the pivots in columns 1 and 2.
Put a box around columns 1 and 2 of the original matrix A.
Write x_3=s and x_4=t to show where the null-space parameters come from.
Draw a simple split: pivot columns describe Col(A), free variables describe Nul(A).

walkthrough.label: Find pivot columns
walkthrough.latex: The pivots are in columns 1 and 2 of the RREF. So the pivot columns of the original matrix are the first two columns of A.

walkthrough.label: Solve Ax=0 using the RREF
walkthrough.latex: The RREF gives\n\[x_1+2x_3-x_4=0,\qquad x_2+x_3+2x_4=0.\]\nLet \(x_3=s\) and \(x_4=t\). Then \(x_1=-2s+t\) and \(x_2=-s-2t\).

walkthrough.label: Rank and nullity
walkthrough.latex: There are 2 pivots, so \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=2\). There are 2 free variables, so \(\operatorname{nullity}(A)=2\). Check: \(2+2=4\), the number of columns.

commonMistakes: Using columns 1 and 2 of the RREF as the Col(A) basis instead of original A.
Forgetting that the null-space basis vectors must have 4 entries.
Calling the zero rows the nullity. Nullity is the number of free variables.

practiceLabPointer.note: More practice with column space, null space, bases, and dimensions.

problems.title: Coordinates in a nonstandard basis
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let \(B=\{b_1,b_2,b_3\}\) where
\[b_1=\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{bmatrix},\quad b_2=\begin{bmatrix}2\\0\\1\end{bmatrix},\quad b_3=\begin{bmatrix}0\\3\\-1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Decide whether \(B\) is a basis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\). Then find \([v]_B\) for \(v=\begin{bmatrix}5\\-1\\3\end{bmatrix}\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: coordinates in B are not the same as standard coordinates. We are solving for the weights on the basis vectors.

drawInstructions: Draw three independent directions from the origin labeled b1, b2, b3.
Draw v as a target vector.
Write v = c1 b1 + c2 b2 + c3 b3.
Emphasize that [v]_B is the list of coefficients, not the same drawing as v.

walkthrough.label: Check if B is a basis
walkthrough.latex: \[\det(P)=-1\neq 0.\]\nSo the columns are independent and span \(\mathbb{R}^3\). Therefore \(B\) is a basis.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to put basis vectors as columns.
Thinking [v]_B means the same thing as v.
Stopping after det is nonzero and forgetting to find the coordinate vector.

practiceLabPointer.note: Good follow-up for basis and coordinate practice.

problems.title: Basis and dimension of a solution subspace
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let
\[W=\{(x,y,z,w)\in\mathbb{R}^4: x+y-z=0,\; 2x+y+w=0\}.\]
Find a basis for \(W\) and find \(\dim(W)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this is a homogeneous system, so the solution set is a subspace. Then we parametrize it.

drawInstructions: Do not try to draw R4 exactly.
Draw a small parameter table with x=s and y=t.
Show that z and w are controlled by s and t.
Say this subspace has two independent directions, so dimension 2.

walkthrough.label: Use the equations to solve for z and w
walkthrough.latex: From \(x+y-z=0\), we get \(z=x+y\). From \(2x+y+w=0\), we get \(w=-2x-y\).

walkthrough.label: Read the basis and dimension
walkthrough.latex: A basis is\n\[\{(1,0,1,-2),(0,1,1,-1)\}.\]\nThere are two basis vectors, so \(\dim(W)=2\).

commonMistakes: Trying to draw R4 literally instead of using parameters.
Forgetting that the vector has four components.
Not checking that the equations are homogeneous before calling it a subspace.

practiceLabPointer.note: More basis/dimension problems are in Practice Lab Section 3.

wrapUpQuestions: For Col(A), do we use the original matrix or the RREF columns?
What is the difference between v and [v]_B?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 3
linksToWebsite.reason: More practice with bases, dimensions, column space, and null space.

qualityControlNotes: Problem 1 checked: column 3 = 2 column 1 + column 2, column 4 = -column 1 + 2 column 2, so the given RREF is consistent with the original matrix.
Problem 1 null-space basis checked: vectors (-2,-1,1,0) and (1,-2,0,1) satisfy the RREF equations.
Problem 2 checked: determinant of the basis matrix is -1 and v=-b1+3b2.
Problem 3 checked: z=x+y and w=-2x-y gives basis vectors (1,0,1,-2) and (0,1,1,-1).
reviewSummary: Session 06 uses simple language around bases, dimension, column space, and null space. Arithmetic and notation were checked before export.
